title: Moroccan chicken with preserved lemon
subtitle: One-pan roasted chicken thighs with ras-el-hanout, olives, and preserved lemon
excerpt: Chicken thighs rubbed with ras-el-hanout and roasted in a single pan with garlic, preserved lemon, and green olives. The pan juices reduce around the chicken to form a concentrated, fragrant sauce. Less laborious than a full tagine and on the table in under an hour.
sourceNotes: This one-pan preparation draws on the Moroccan tradition of roasting chicken with preserved lemon and olives. The combination is documented in Moroccan cookery accounts from the nineteenth century. Paula Wolfert's The Food of Morocco provides extensive documentation of the preserved lemon tradition. This version uses ras-el-hanout for convenience in place of individual spices.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: The chicken can be rubbed with the spice mix up to 4 hours ahead and refrigerated. Bring to room temperature before roasting.

content.text: This is the weeknight version of the chicken tagine — a single pan in the oven rather than a long braise on the hob. The chicken goes in skin-side up to roast rather than braise, so the skin crisps while the pan juices bubble down around the olives and preserved lemon. The result is not the same dish as a slow-braised tagine, but it is faster and comes with a crispier result.

Preserved lemons are sold in jars in most supermarkets. Use the rind only — the flesh inside is discarded. Rinse the rind to remove excess salt before slicing. The olives can be pitted or not, but pitting them makes the dish easier to eat.

items.prepNote: preserved, rind rinsed and sliced, flesh discarded

content.text: Season the chicken and heat the oven

Heat the oven to 200°C (fan 180°C). Rub the {{chicken-thigh}} chicken thighs all over with the {{ras-el-hanout}} of ras-el-hanout and {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt. Pour the {{extra-virgin-olive-oil}} of olive oil into a roasting pan and arrange the chicken skin-side up.

Scatter the {{garlic}} of unpeeled garlic cloves, sliced rind of the {{lemon}} preserved lemon, and {{olives-green}} of olives around the chicken. Pour the {{stock-chicken}} of stock into the base of the pan. Roast for 40 to 45 minutes until the skin is deep golden and crisp and the juices run clear when the thickest part is pierced. Rest for 5 minutes. Scatter over {{parsley-flat}} of parsley and serve with couscous or flatbreads.

items.cause: Pan too crowded, or temperature too low
items.fix: Use a large roasting pan so thighs are not touching; ensure the oven is fully preheated; if needed, finish under the grill for 3 to 4 minutes

items.cause: Too little stock or oven too hot
items.fix: Add a splash of water to the pan mid-cook; the juices should simmer, not evaporate completely
Rinse the preserved lemon rind well under cold water before slicing; it is packed in salt and will make the dish very salty without rinsing

content.text: Preserved lemons are one of the most distinctive ingredients in the Moroccan kitchen — and one of the simplest to make at home, though they take a month of salt-curing before they are ready. The salt draws out the moisture, the juice ferments slightly, and the rind softens into something that tastes nothing like fresh lemon: floral, salty, faintly funky. The roast-chicken method here is an adaptation of the traditional braised approach into something that suits a domestic oven. It trades the falling-off-the-bone tenderness of the tagine for a crispy skin, which is a fair exchange.
